records.ety: [F., fr. L. imago, imaginis, from the root of imitari to imitate. See Imitate, and cf. Imagine.]

defs: An imitation, representation, or similitude of any person, thing, or act, sculptured, drawn, painted, or otherwise made perceptible to the sight; a visible presentation; a copy; a likeness; an effigy; a picture; a semblance.
quotes: Even like a stony image, cold and numb.
Whose is this image and superscription?
This play is the image of a murder done in Vienna.
And God created man in his own image.

defs: Hence: The likeness of anything to which worship is paid; an idol.
quotes: Thou shalt not make unto thee any graven image, . . . thou shalt not bow down thyself to them.
The face of things a frightful image bears.

defs: A representation of anything to the mind; a picture drawn by the fancy; a conception; an idea.
quotes: Can we conceive Image of aught delightful, soft, or great?

defs: A picture, example, or illustration, often taken from sensible objects, and used to illustrate a subject; usually, an extended metaphor.

The figure or picture of any object formed at the focus of a lens or mirror, by rays of light from the several points of the object symmetrically refracted or reflected to corresponding points in such focus; this may be received on a screen, a photographic plate, or the retina of the eye, and viewed directly by the eye, or with an eyeglass, as in the telescope and microscope; the likeness of an object formed by reflection; as, to see one's image in a mirror.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Imaged (m"jd; 48); p. pr. & vb. n. Imaging (?).]

defs: To represent or form an image of; as, the still lake imaged the shore; the mirror imaged her figure.
To represent to the mental vision; to form a likeness of by the fancy or recollection; to imagine.
quotes: Condemn'd whole years in absence to deplore, And image charms he must behold no more.
